sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A primary-care clinic screened adults for depressive symptoms and asked about treatment barriers. Patients with elevated scores commonly reported low energy, sleep disruption, and loss of interest. Many also reported that cost, transportation, and fear of being judged made it harder to attend follow-up counseling. Clinicians noted that symptom severity and structural barriers both affected whether a patient received care after a positive screen.
questions.stem: According to the passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.A: Elevated depression symptoms were paired with both clinical and access barriers
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.A: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: Elevated depression symptoms were paired with both clinical and access barriers. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.B: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.topic: Major Depressive Symptoms And Treatment Access

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

choices.C: major depressive symptoms and treatment access

explanations.C: Correct. The scenario is best explained by major depressive symptoms and treatment access because the key issue is that Symptoms such as low energy can reduce follow-through, while cost and transportation make care harder to obtain. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.topic: Major Depressive Symptoms And Treatment Access

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.B: Symptoms such as low energy can reduce follow-through, while cost and transportation make care harder to obtain.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.C: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.B: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Symptoms such as low energy can reduce follow-through, while cost and transportation make care harder to obtain. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.C: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.topic: Major Depressive Symptoms And Treatment Access

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.D: Integrate screening with low-cost referrals, transportation help, and stigma-sensitive follow-up.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.B: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.C: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.D: Correct. The best application is to Integrate screening with low-cost referrals, transportation help, and stigma-sensitive follow-up. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.B: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.C: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.B: A positive screen is not the same as a definitive diagnosis and does not isolate one cause of missed care.
choices.A: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.C: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.B: Correct. The key limitation is that A positive screen is not the same as a definitive diagnosis and does not isolate one cause of missed care. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.A: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.C: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.

questions.passage: A clinic evaluated two approaches for patients with specific phobia: waitlist monitoring and CBT with gradual exposure. After eight weeks, mean avoidance scores fell from 8.4 to 7.9 in the waitlist group and from 8.6 to 4.2 in the CBT group. Self-efficacy scores rose from 3.1 to 3.4 in waitlist patients and from 3.0 to 6.8 in CBT patients. Therapists emphasized repeated exposure without the feared outcome.
questions.stem: According to the figure and passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.A: CBT with gradual exposure produced the larger reduction in avoidance
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.A: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: CBT with gradual exposure produced the larger reduction in avoidance. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.B: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Avoidance and self-efficacy outcomes for waitlist versus CBT with exposure.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.C: Correct. The scenario is best explained by exposure-based CBT for anxiety because the key issue is that Repeated safe exposure can weaken fear associations and increase confidence in coping with the feared stimulus. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.D: Repeated safe exposure can weaken fear associations and increase confidence in coping with the feared stimulus.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.B: A complete absence of social context
choices.C: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.D: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Repeated safe exposure can weaken fear associations and increase confidence in coping with the feared stimulus. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.B: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.C: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.A: Use structured gradual exposure rather than avoidance-only reassurance.
choices.B: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.C: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.D: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.A: Correct. The best application is to Use structured gradual exposure rather than avoidance-only reassurance. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.B: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.C: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.D: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.C: If patients selected treatment rather than being randomly assigned, motivation could contribute to the difference.
choices.A: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.B: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.C: Correct. The key limitation is that If patients selected treatment rather than being randomly assigned, motivation could contribute to the difference. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.A: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.B: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.
